text: During laparoscopy, the liver is retracted using a percutaneous stay suture in the falciform ligament, with an additional suture placed at the right hepatic lobe for better exposure. The surgeon visually confirms the exclusion of intestinal malrotation under laparoscopic vision.

The surgeon identifies the duodenum and mobilizes the right colon at the hepatic flexure, exposing the dilated upper duodenum and the inferior duodenum. A gentle dissection is performed using a laparoscopic approach to free the jejunum.

The surgeon suspends the upper end of the duodenum using a percutaneous suture, facilitating the dissection of the distal duodenum, which appears to have a smaller diameter. Care is taken to ensure no other intestinal structures mimic the distal end of the duodenum.

The surgeon makes a transverse incision in the upper duodenum using laparoscopic scissors, followed by advancing the orogastric tube under laparoscopic guidance to ensure no duodenal webs are present. Subsequently, the inferior duodenum is incised longitudinally to maintain the anastomosis's caliber and patency.

The surgeon performs an Akimura diamond anastomosis using a polydioxanone suture, starting at the left corner of the transverse incision and extending to the midpoint of the longitudinal incision in the distal duodenum. The suture is placed percutaneously, with both ends exteriorized, and the process is repeated on the right side. The suspension of the upper duodenum is then loosened to achieve a more physiological and less tense anastomosis.

The surgeon creates the posterior side of the anastomosis using extracorporeal laparoscopic interrupted knots, beginning at the midline. The anterior side of the anastomosis is completed using the same technique, while the lateral knots are tied intracorporeally.

The surgeon tests the patency and impermeability of the anastomosis by injecting twenty milliliters of saline solution and air through the orogastric tube. A closed drain is placed and exteriorized through a five millimeter port, while the orogastric tube remains in place to decompress the stomach and monitor motility.
